'''Main enshrined deity''' * '''Omurakami'' (Omurakami) *: In the shrine biography, the 11th {{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850}}{{ill|lt=Prince|Q12886381|WD=Q12886381}}'s {{ill|lt=Kisokubetsumei|Q11492324|WD=Q11492324}} (Ikohayawake no Mikoto/Okihayawake no Mikoto) is applied to this. '''Deity enshrined'' * {{ill|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Kyotsu no Mikoto|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|lt=Kyotsu no Mikoto}} (Futsunushi no Mikoto) * {{ill|lt=Amagiya Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Amagoyane Mikoto}} (Amenokoyane Mikoto) '''Combined deities''' * Total 17 pillars including {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}} === About the enshrined deity === The enshrined deity is listed as 1 in ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}'' {{ill|lt=Engishiki divine name book|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=God name book}}. On the other hand, in the ``Kofukuji Kanjusho'' written in the first year of {{ill|lt=Kakichi|Q1193238|WD=Q1193238}} ({{ill|lt=1441|Q6402|WD=Q6402}}), ``Omurakami It is written as "Niza", and {{ill|lt=Kasugagami|Q11088805|WD=Q11088805}} was born in {{ill|lt=Jingo Keiun|Q1373049|WD=Q1373049}} in 2nd year ({{ill|lt=768|Q29961|WD=Q29961}}). It is said to be the place where he rested when he moved from Jingu Shrine | Q706499}} to {{ill|lt=Mikasayama|Q10866570|WD=Q10866570}} ({{ill|lt=Kasuga Taisha|Q714559|WD=Q714559}}) {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. The current enshrined deities are {{ill|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Tsunetsu no Mikoto|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|lt=Tsunetsu no Mikoto}}, {{jalin k|Amagiya-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|Amagoyane-no-Mikoto}} and the other three Kasuga deities originate from this transfer tradition, but its historical reality is unclear {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}.On the other hand, {{ill|lt=Bokusokubetsu no Mikoto|Q11492324|WD=Q11492324}}, who is currently designated as Omura-kami, is a person whose relationship with the Abo area can be seen in ancient historical materials {{Sfn|Omura-jinja Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. First of all, in the article "{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihongi|Q611399|WD=Q611399}}" dated November 21, {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=784 years|Q30083|WD=Q30083}}), the speed of breathing is {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}} who lived in Abo Village, Iga Province, and whose fourth grandson, King Sune Tsuto (Suchin Tsuto King), was given the surname "{{ill|lt=Abo clan|Q11657230|WD=Q11657230|lt=Abo-kun}}". Also, “{{ill|lt=Newly Selected Surname List|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362}}” by Ukyo Emperor In the Abo Ason Article, it is written that Emperor Suinin created a palace in Abo Village, Iga Province and granted it to Isokubetsu no Mikoto as a fief, and that his descendants became Abo Ason ({{ill|lt=Abo clan|Q11657230|WD=Q11657230}}) {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. Near Omura Shrine, there is also a tomb designated by the Imperial Household Agency, which according to recent archeology dates back to {{ill|lt=6th century|Q8090|WD=Q8090}}, so there is a theory that one of the tombs (Miyayama Tumulus Group) within the precincts of Omura Shrine is the tomb of Seisokubetsu no Mikoto.{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}.In addition to the above, there is also a theory that the enshrined deity is Yuki Isun or Daimyo Somei ({{ill|lt=Omura clan (Kamibetsu)|Q11436025|WD=Q11436025|lt=Nao Omura}} ancestor) {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. The 17 deities enshrined together are the 28 shrines of the former Abo town and 3 shrines of the former Taneo village that were enshrined between 1890 and 1986 ({{ill|lt=1890|Q7832|WD=Q7832}}-{{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056}}). === History === === Founding === Founding date is unknown. As mentioned above, there is a legend about the transfer of Kasuga God from 2 years {{ill|lt=Jingo Keiun|Q1373049|WD=Q1373049}} ({{ill|lt=768 years|Q29961|WD=Q29961}}) (first seen in historical sources is {{ill|lt=1441|Q6402|WD=Q6402}}) ), there is a theory that assumes the involvement of the {{ill|lt=Abo clan|Q11657230|WD=Q11657230}} (a descendant clan of the Breathless Gods) who made this place {{ill|lt=honkan|Q846706|WD=Q846706}}{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. Regarding the company's name, there is a theory that it is a corruption of ``Omori'', which refers to a group of shrines, to ``Omura'', since it looks like ``Omori Shrine/Omori Myojin'' in ancient times, or that it is a corruption of the place name ``Abo Village'', which is a corruption of ``Omura'', but the name is unknown.{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. === Outline === In {{ill|lt=Rokukokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168|lt=Kokukushi}}, {{ill|lt=Jōkan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}}5 ({{ill|lt=863|Q25052|WD=Q25052}}) There is a record that the {{ill|lt=jinka|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025}} of ``Omurakami'' was promoted from the upper rank of upper sixth rank to lower rank of junior fifth rank {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1990}}. {{ill|lt=extension (Era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extended}} 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}) "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}" {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shinmyocho}} is described as "Omura Shrine" in {{ill|lt=Iga Province|Q1047128|WD=Q1047128}}{{ill|lt=Iga District|Q11380682|WD=Q11380682}}, and is listed in {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchi|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}}{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1990}}. Also, among the place names that appear in ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Japanese name extract|1=UNKNOWN}}'', the current enshrined place is thought to be Abogo, Iga District{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}.According to "Sangokujishi", the shrine suffered a fire in {{ill|lt=Eisho|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577}} years ({{ill|lt=1504|Q6148|WD=Q6148}}-{{ill|lt=1521|Q6288|WD=Q6288}}), and then in {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}}9 years ({{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}}). {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}} was destroyed by fire during the {{ill|lt=Tensho Iga Rebellion|Q15436846|WD=Q15436846}} in {{ill|lt=1581|Q6717|WD=Q6717}}), but was rebuilt in Tensho 15 ({{ill|lt=1587|Q6744|WD=Q6744}}). The main shrine built at this time still remains today as a treasure hall (national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}}). In this {{ill|lt=ridge tag|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834}} dated 15th year of Tensho, the company name is "Kashima Daimyojin" and {{ill|lt=Genwa (Japan)|Q635245|WD=Q635245|lt=Genna}}7th year ({{ill|lt=1621|Q6812|WD=Q6812}}), {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yasunaga|1=UNKNOWN}}8th year ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=1779|1=UNKNOWN}})'s ridge tag also appears as ``Kashima Shrine,'' so it is known that the legend of the transfer of Kasuga Shrine was well-established during this period {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Furthermore, the Akinaga clan had been in charge of the Shinto priesthood since Tensho 15 (1990).After {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, on August 7, {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 17 ({{ill|lt=1884|Q7819|WD=Q7819}}), he was ranked as {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} in {{ill|lt=Modern Company Ranking System|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}, and in Meiji 39 In December 2006 ({{ill|lt=1906|Q2049|WD=Q2049}}), it was designated as a Shinto shrine, and in 5 years ({{j alink|1930|Q18787}}) On November 27th, it was promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226}}. {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1990}}. {{ill|lt=Bettoji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755}} was once a Zenjoji temple, but it was abandoned in the first year of the Meiji era {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. === God level === * {{ill|lt=Jogan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jogan}} 5th year ({{ill|lt=863|Q25052|WD=Q25052}}) March 16th, from upper 6th rank to lower junior fifth rank (``{{ill|lt=Japan Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981}}'') - Written as ``Omurakami.'' === Precincts === The current ``main shrine'' was built in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}23 ({{ill|lt=1890|Q7832|WD=Q7832}}){{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. It is {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781}}, and the roof is {{ill|lt=cypress bark|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477}}.{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1990}}. The old main hall, which was used before the current main hall, is adjacent to the west side of the current main hall as the ``Hoden''. This Hoden was built in the 15th year of {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} ({{ill|lt=1587|Q6744|WD=Q6744}}) of the {{ill|lt=Azuchi-Momoyama period|Q319531|WD=Q319531}}. Ikkensha {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025}}, with a tsumairi roof, {{ill|lt=cypress bark|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477}}. It is said that the form as well as the carvings and coloring remain as they were at the time of construction, and it has been designated as a national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}}.In addition, within the precincts there is a {{ill|lt=Keystone|Q11630053|WD=Q11630053}}, which is worshiped as a spiritual stone, and the strange bell ``Mushikui-kane'' {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. The name "Insect-eaten bell" comes from the fact that the bumps on its surface fell off due to a chemical change, but it was originally a temple bell at Zenjoji Temple (formerly Bettoji Temple) and was completed in {{ill|lt=Meireki|Q1192766|WD=Q1192766}}2 ({{ill|lt=1656|Q6982|WD=Q6982}}) {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Also, from the approach to the shrine grounds to the vicinity of the shrine building, there are several {{ill|lt=circular tombs|Q11394747|WD=Q11394747}} known as the ``Miyayama kofun group''{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. '''Main enshrined deity''' * '''Omurakami'' (Omurakami) *: In the shrine biography, the 11th {{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850}}{{ill|lt=Prince|Q12886381|WD=Q12886381}}'s {{ill|lt=Kisokubetsumei|Q11492324|WD=Q11492324}} (Ikohayawake no Mikoto/Okihayawake no Mikoto) is applied to this. '''Deity enshrined'' * {{ill|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Kyotsu no Mikoto|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|lt=Kyotsu no Mikoto}} (Futsunushi no Mikoto) * {{ill|lt=Amagiya Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Amagoyane Mikoto}} (Amenokoyane Mikoto) '''Combined deities''' * Total 17 pillars including {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}} === About the enshrined deity === The enshrined deity is listed as 1 in ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}'' {{ill|lt=Engishiki divine name book|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=God name book}}. On the other hand, in the ``Kofukuji Kanjusho'' written in the first year of {{ill|lt=Kakichi|Q1193238|WD=Q1193238}} ({{ill|lt=1441|Q6402|WD=Q6402}}), ``Omurakami It is written as "Niza", and {{ill|lt=Kasugagami|Q11088805|WD=Q11088805}} was born in {{ill|lt=Jingo Keiun|Q1373049|WD=Q1373049}} in 2nd year ({{ill|lt=768|Q29961|WD=Q29961}}). It is said to be the place where he rested when he moved from Jingu Shrine | Q706499}} to {{ill|lt=Mikasayama|Q10866570|WD=Q10866570}} ({{ill|lt=Kasuga Taisha|Q714559|WD=Q714559}}) {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. The current enshrined deities are {{ill|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Tsunetsu no Mikoto|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|lt=Tsunetsu no Mikoto}}, {{jalin k|Amagiya-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|Amagoyane-no-Mikoto}} and the other three Kasuga deities originate from this transfer tradition, but its historical reality is unclear {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}.On the other hand, {{ill|lt=Bokusokubetsu no Mikoto|Q11492324|WD=Q11492324}}, who is currently designated as Omura-kami, is a person whose relationship with the Abo area can be seen in ancient historical materials {{Sfn|Omura-jinja Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. First of all, in the article "{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihongi|Q611399|WD=Q611399}}" dated November 21, {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=784 years|Q30083|WD=Q30083}}), the speed of breathing is {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}} who lived in Abo Village, Iga Province, and whose fourth grandson, King Sune Tsuto (Suchin Tsuto King), was given the surname "{{ill|lt=Abo clan|Q11657230|WD=Q11657230|lt=Abo-kun}}". Also, “{{ill|lt=Newly Selected Surname List|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362}}” by Ukyo Emperor In the Abo Ason Article, it is written that Emperor Suinin created a palace in Abo Village, Iga Province and granted it to Isokubetsu no Mikoto as a fief, and that his descendants became Abo Ason ({{ill|lt=Abo clan|Q11657230|WD=Q11657230}}) {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. Near Omura Shrine, there is also a tomb designated by the Imperial Household Agency, which according to recent archeology dates back to {{ill|lt=6th century|Q8090|WD=Q8090}}, so there is a theory that one of the tombs (Miyayama Tumulus Group) within the precincts of Omura Shrine is the tomb of Seisokubetsu no Mikoto.{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}.In addition to the above, there is also a theory that the enshrined deity is Yuki Isun or Daimyo Somei ({{ill|lt=Omura clan (Kamibetsu)|Q11436025|WD=Q11436025|lt=Nao Omura}} ancestor) {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. The 17 deities enshrined together are the 28 shrines of the former Abo town and 3 shrines of the former Taneo village that were enshrined between 1890 and 1986 ({{ill|lt=1890|Q7832|WD=Q7832}}-{{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056}}). === History === === Founding === Founding date is unknown. As mentioned above, there is a legend about the transfer of Kasuga God from 2 years {{ill|lt=Jingo Keiun|Q1373049|WD=Q1373049}} ({{ill|lt=768 years|Q29961|WD=Q29961}}) (first seen in historical sources is {{ill|lt=1441|Q6402|WD=Q6402}}) ), there is a theory that assumes the involvement of the {{ill|lt=Abo clan|Q11657230|WD=Q11657230}} (a descendant clan of the Breathless Gods) who made this place {{ill|lt=honkan|Q846706|WD=Q846706}}{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. Regarding the company's name, there is a theory that it is a corruption of ``Omori'', which refers to a group of shrines, to ``Omura'', since it looks like ``Omori Shrine/Omori Myojin'' in ancient times, or that it is a corruption of the place name ``Abo Village'', which is a corruption of ``Omura'', but the name is unknown.{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. === Outline === In {{ill|lt=Rokukokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168|lt=Kokukushi}}, {{ill|lt=Jōkan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}}5 ({{ill|lt=863|Q25052|WD=Q25052}}) There is a record that the {{ill|lt=jinka|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025}} of ``Omurakami'' was promoted from the upper rank of upper sixth rank to lower rank of junior fifth rank {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1990}}. {{ill|lt=extension (Era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extended}} 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}) "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}" {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shinmyocho}} is described as "Omura Shrine" in {{ill|lt=Iga Province|Q1047128|WD=Q1047128}}{{ill|lt=Iga District|Q11380682|WD=Q11380682}}, and is listed in {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchi|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}}{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1990}}. Also, among the place names that appear in ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Japanese name extract|1=UNKNOWN}}'', the current enshrined place is thought to be Abogo, Iga District{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}.According to "Sangokujishi", the shrine suffered a fire in {{ill|lt=Eisho|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577}} years ({{ill|lt=1504|Q6148|WD=Q6148}}-{{ill|lt=1521|Q6288|WD=Q6288}}), and then in {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}}9 years ({{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}}). {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}} was destroyed by fire during the {{ill|lt=Tensho Iga Rebellion|Q15436846|WD=Q15436846}} in {{ill|lt=1581|Q6717|WD=Q6717}}), but was rebuilt in Tensho 15 ({{ill|lt=1587|Q6744|WD=Q6744}}). The main shrine built at this time still remains today as a treasure hall (national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}}). In this {{ill|lt=ridge tag|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834}} dated 15th year of Tensho, the company name is "Kashima Daimyojin" and {{ill|lt=Genwa (Japan)|Q635245|WD=Q635245|lt=Genna}}7th year ({{ill|lt=1621|Q6812|WD=Q6812}}), {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yasunaga|1=UNKNOWN}}8th year ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=1779|1=UNKNOWN}})'s ridge tag also appears as ``Kashima Shrine,'' so it is known that the legend of the transfer of Kasuga Shrine was well-established during this period {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Furthermore, the Akinaga clan had been in charge of the Shinto priesthood since Tensho 15 (1990).After {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, on August 7, {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 17 ({{ill|lt=1884|Q7819|WD=Q7819}}), he was ranked as {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} in {{ill|lt=Modern Company Ranking System|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}, and in Meiji 39 In December 2006 ({{ill|lt=1906|Q2049|WD=Q2049}}), it was designated as a Shinto shrine, and in 5 years ({{j alink|1930|Q18787}}) On November 27th, it was promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226}}. {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1990}}. {{ill|lt=Bettoji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755}} was once a Zenjoji temple, but it was abandoned in the first year of the Meiji era {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. === God level === * {{ill|lt=Jogan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jogan}} 5th year ({{ill|lt=863|Q25052|WD=Q25052}}) March 16th, from upper 6th rank to lower junior fifth rank (``{{ill|lt=Japan Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981}}'') - Written as ``Omurakami.'' === Precincts === The current ``main shrine'' was built in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}23 ({{ill|lt=1890|Q7832|WD=Q7832}}){{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. It is {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781}}, and the roof is {{ill|lt=cypress bark|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477}}.{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1990}}. The old main hall, which was used before the current main hall, is adjacent to the west side of the current main hall as the ``Hoden''. This Hoden was built in the 15th year of {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} ({{ill|lt=1587|Q6744|WD=Q6744}}) of the {{ill|lt=Azuchi-Momoyama period|Q319531|WD=Q319531}}. Ikkensha {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025}}, with a tsumairi roof, {{ill|lt=cypress bark|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477}}. It is said that the form as well as the carvings and coloring remain as they were at the time of construction, and it has been designated as a national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}}.In addition, within the precincts there is a {{ill|lt=Keystone|Q11630053|WD=Q11630053}}, which is worshiped as a spiritual stone, and the strange bell ``Mushikui-kane'' {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. The name "Insect-eaten bell" comes from the fact that the bumps on its surface fell off due to a chemical change, but it was originally a temple bell at Zenjoji Temple (formerly Bettoji Temple) and was completed in {{ill|lt=Meireki|Q1192766|WD=Q1192766}}2 ({{ill|lt=1656|Q6982|WD=Q6982}}) {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Also, from the approach to the shrine grounds to the vicinity of the shrine building, there are several {{ill|lt=circular tombs|Q11394747|WD=Q11394747}} known as the ``Miyayama kofun group''{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. '''Main enshrined deity''' * '''Omurakami'' (Omurakami) *: In the shrine biography, the 11th {{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850}}{{ill|lt=Prince|Q12886381|WD=Q12886381}}'s {{ill|lt=Kisokubetsumei|Q11492324|WD=Q11492324}} (Ikohayawake no Mikoto/Okihayawake no Mikoto) is applied to this. '''Deity enshrined'' * {{ill|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Kyotsu no Mikoto|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|lt=Kyotsu no Mikoto}} (Futsunushi no Mikoto) * {{ill|lt=Amagiya Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Amagoyane Mikoto}} (Amenokoyane Mikoto) '''Combined deities''' * Total 17 pillars including {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}} === About the enshrined deity === The enshrined deity is listed as 1 in ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}'' {{ill|lt=Engishiki divine name book|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=God name book}}. On the other hand, in the ``Kofukuji Kanjusho'' written in the first year of {{ill|lt=Kakichi|Q1193238|WD=Q1193238}} ({{ill|lt=1441|Q6402|WD=Q6402}}), ``Omurakami It is written as "Niza", and {{ill|lt=Kasugagami|Q11088805|WD=Q11088805}} was born in {{ill|lt=Jingo Keiun|Q1373049|WD=Q1373049}} in 2nd year ({{ill|lt=768|Q29961|WD=Q29961}}). It is said to be the place where he rested when he moved from Jingu Shrine | Q706499}} to {{ill|lt=Mikasayama|Q10866570|WD=Q10866570}} ({{ill|lt=Kasuga Taisha|Q714559|WD=Q714559}}) {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. The current enshrined deities are {{ill|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Tsunetsu no Mikoto|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|lt=Tsunetsu no Mikoto}}, {{jalin k|Amagiya-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|Amagoyane-no-Mikoto}} and the other three Kasuga deities originate from this transfer tradition, but its historical reality is unclear {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}.On the other hand, {{ill|lt=Bokusokubetsu no Mikoto|Q11492324|WD=Q11492324}}, who is currently designated as Omura-kami, is a person whose relationship with the Abo area can be seen in ancient historical materials {{Sfn|Omura-jinja Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. First of all, in the article "{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihongi|Q611399|WD=Q611399}}" dated November 21, {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=784 years|Q30083|WD=Q30083}}), the speed of breathing is {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}} who lived in Abo Village, Iga Province, and whose fourth grandson, King Sune Tsuto (Suchin Tsuto King), was given the surname "{{ill|lt=Abo clan|Q11657230|WD=Q11657230|lt=Abo-kun}}". Also, “{{ill|lt=Newly Selected Surname List|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362}}” by Ukyo Emperor In the Abo Ason Article, it is written that Emperor Suinin created a palace in Abo Village, Iga Province and granted it to Isokubetsu no Mikoto as a fief, and that his descendants became Abo Ason ({{ill|lt=Abo clan|Q11657230|WD=Q11657230}}) {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. Near Omura Shrine, there is also a tomb designated by the Imperial Household Agency, which according to recent archeology dates back to {{ill|lt=6th century|Q8090|WD=Q8090}}, so there is a theory that one of the tombs (Miyayama Tumulus Group) within the precincts of Omura Shrine is the tomb of Seisokubetsu no Mikoto.{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}.In addition to the above, there is also a theory that the enshrined deity is Yuki Isun or Daimyo Somei ({{ill|lt=Omura clan (Kamibetsu)|Q11436025|WD=Q11436025|lt=Nao Omura}} ancestor) {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. The 17 deities enshrined together are the 28 shrines of the former Abo town and 3 shrines of the former Taneo village that were enshrined between 1890 and 1986 ({{ill|lt=1890|Q7832|WD=Q7832}}-{{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056}}). === History === === Founding === Founding date is unknown. As mentioned above, there is a legend about the transfer of Kasuga God from 2 years {{ill|lt=Jingo Keiun|Q1373049|WD=Q1373049}} ({{ill|lt=768 years|Q29961|WD=Q29961}}) (first seen in historical sources is {{ill|lt=1441|Q6402|WD=Q6402}}) ), there is a theory that assumes the involvement of the {{ill|lt=Abo clan|Q11657230|WD=Q11657230}} (a descendant clan of the Breathless Gods) who made this place {{ill|lt=honkan|Q846706|WD=Q846706}}{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. Regarding the company's name, there is a theory that it is a corruption of ``Omori'', which refers to a group of shrines, to ``Omura'', since it looks like ``Omori Shrine/Omori Myojin'' in ancient times, or that it is a corruption of the place name ``Abo Village'', which is a corruption of ``Omura'', but the name is unknown.{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. === Outline === In {{ill|lt=Rokukokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168|lt=Kokukushi}}, {{ill|lt=Jōkan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}}5 ({{ill|lt=863|Q25052|WD=Q25052}}) There is a record that the {{ill|lt=jinka|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025}} of ``Omurakami'' was promoted from the upper rank of upper sixth rank to lower rank of junior fifth rank {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1990}}. {{ill|lt=extension (Era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extended}} 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}) "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}" {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shinmyocho}} is described as "Omura Shrine" in {{ill|lt=Iga Province|Q1047128|WD=Q1047128}}{{ill|lt=Iga District|Q11380682|WD=Q11380682}}, and is listed in {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchi|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}}{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1990}}. Also, among the place names that appear in ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Japanese name extract|1=UNKNOWN}}'', the current enshrined place is thought to be Abogo, Iga District{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}.According to "Sangokujishi", the shrine suffered a fire in {{ill|lt=Eisho|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577}} years ({{ill|lt=1504|Q6148|WD=Q6148}}-{{ill|lt=1521|Q6288|WD=Q6288}}), and then in {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}}9 years ({{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}}). {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}} was destroyed by fire during the {{ill|lt=Tensho Iga Rebellion|Q15436846|WD=Q15436846}} in {{ill|lt=1581|Q6717|WD=Q6717}}), but was rebuilt in Tensho 15 ({{ill|lt=1587|Q6744|WD=Q6744}}). The main shrine built at this time still remains today as a treasure hall (national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}}). In this {{ill|lt=ridge tag|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834}} dated 15th year of Tensho, the company name is "Kashima Daimyojin" and {{ill|lt=Genwa (Japan)|Q635245|WD=Q635245|lt=Genna}}7th year ({{ill|lt=1621|Q6812|WD=Q6812}}), {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yasunaga|1=UNKNOWN}}8th year ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=1779|1=UNKNOWN}})'s ridge tag also appears as ``Kashima Shrine,'' so it is known that the legend of the transfer of Kasuga Shrine was well-established during this period {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Furthermore, the Akinaga clan had been in charge of the Shinto priesthood since Tensho 15 (1990).After {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, on August 7, {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 17 ({{ill|lt=1884|Q7819|WD=Q7819}}), he was ranked as {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} in {{ill|lt=Modern Company Ranking System|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}, and in Meiji 39 In December 2006 ({{ill|lt=1906|Q2049|WD=Q2049}}), it was designated as a Shinto shrine, and in 5 years ({{j alink|1930|Q18787}}) On November 27th, it was promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226}}. {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1990}}. {{ill|lt=Bettoji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755}} was once a Zenjoji temple, but it was abandoned in the first year of the Meiji era {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. === God level === * {{ill|lt=Jogan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jogan}} 5th year ({{ill|lt=863|Q25052|WD=Q25052}}) March 16th, from upper 6th rank to lower junior fifth rank (``{{ill|lt=Japan Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981}}'') - Written as ``Omurakami.'' === Precincts === The current ``main shrine'' was built in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}23 ({{ill|lt=1890|Q7832|WD=Q7832}}){{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. It is {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781}}, and the roof is {{ill|lt=cypress bark|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477}}.{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1990}}. The old main hall, which was used before the current main hall, is adjacent to the west side of the current main hall as the ``Hoden''. This Hoden was built in the 15th year of {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} ({{ill|lt=1587|Q6744|WD=Q6744}}) of the {{ill|lt=Azuchi-Momoyama period|Q319531|WD=Q319531}}. Ikkensha {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025}}, with a tsumairi roof, {{ill|lt=cypress bark|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477}}. It is said that the form as well as the carvings and coloring remain as they were at the time of construction, and it has been designated as a national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}}.In addition, within the precincts there is a {{ill|lt=Keystone|Q11630053|WD=Q11630053}}, which is worshiped as a spiritual stone, and the strange bell ``Mushikui-kane'' {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. The name "Insect-eaten bell" comes from the fact that the bumps on its surface fell off due to a chemical change, but it was originally a temple bell at Zenjoji Temple (formerly Bettoji Temple) and was completed in {{ill|lt=Meireki|Q1192766|WD=Q1192766}}2 ({{ill|lt=1656|Q6982|WD=Q6982}}) {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Also, from the approach to the shrine grounds to the vicinity of the shrine building, there are several {{ill|lt=circular tombs|Q11394747|WD=Q11394747}} known as the ``Miyayama kofun group''{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. File:Omura-jinja (Iga) honden.JPG|Main shrine File:Omura-jinja (Iga) haiden.JPG|House of worship === Setsusha === * Kanameishi Shrine * Heroisha File:Omura-jinja (Iga) Kanameishi-sha.JPG|Kanameishi-sha File:Omura-jinja (Iga) Eirei-sha.JPG|Eireisha === Festival === List of festivals held annually at Omura Shrine . *Monthly festival (1st day of every month) * Saitan Festival (New Year's Day Festival) (January 1st) * First prayer (during New Year) * Prayer festival to ward off evil spirits (February 1st) * Prayer to ward off evil spirits (January to end of February) * Prayer Festival (February 21st) * Spring Festival (April 10th) * Eireisha Festival (August 15th) * Earthquake disaster prevention prayer festival (September 1st) * Jugoya Festival (Jūgoya Festival) *Autumn Festival (November 2nd and 3rd) * Shichi-Go-San prayer (during November) * New Grain Thanksgiving (November 21st) * New Year's Eve Bell (New Year's Eve) === Cultural assets === === Important cultural property (designated by the country) === * Treasure Hall (with 3 ridge plates) (building) - Designated on April 15, 1919 {{Database of nationally designated cultural properties|102|1314|Omura Shrine Treasure Hall}}. ==== Iga City Designated Cultural Property ==== * Temple bell ==== Registered cultural property ==== * Main Hall - Registered in 2020. === Local information === [[File:Tomb of Ikohayawake, haisho.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{Center|Tomb of Ikohayawake ({{ill|lt=Iga City|Q460820|WD=Q460820}}Abo)}}]] '''Location''' * {{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196}}{{ill|lt=Iga City|Q460820|WD=Q460820}}Abo 1555 '''Access''' * Railway: {{ill|lt=Kintetsu Osaka Line|Q1531085|WD=Q1531085}} (Kintetsu) {{ill|lt=Kintetsu Osaka Line|Q1052545|WD=Q1052545|lt=Osaka Line}} {{ill|lt=Aoyamacho Station|Q4778841|WD=Q4778841}} (about 10 minutes walk) '''Surroundings''' * Seisokubetsumei Tomb - Tomb designated by the Imperial Household Agency. * Abotongu Ruins - The legendary site of the Tongu of {{ill|lt=Emperor Jito|Q232026|WD=Q232026}}, {{ill|lt=Emperor Shomu|Q349263|WD=Q349263}}, and {{ill|lt=Saio|Q19841349|WD=Q19841349}}. === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} {{reflist}} === References === * Shrine history book * Encyclopedia ** {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=|year=1983|chapter=Omura Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Japanese historical place name system|Q17213509|WD=Q17213509}} 24 Place names in Mie Prefecture|publisher={{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108}}|isbn=978-4582490244|ref={{Harvid|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}}} ** {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=|year=1983|chapter=Omura Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary|Q4093011|WD=Q4093011}} 24 Mie Prefecture|publisher={{ill|lt=Kadokawa Shoten|Q1054765|WD=Q1054765}}|isbn=978-4040012407|ref={{Harvid|Omura Shrine (Kadokawa)|1983}}}} * Other books ** {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Meiji Shrine Materials Editorial Office|author=|year=1912|chapter=Omura Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Meiji Shrine Materials (伊賀市)]] {{wikidata link|Q20043474}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Omura Shrine |Image = [[File:Omura-jinja (Iga) torii.JPG|280px]]
Torii and worship hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Iga City|Q460820|WD=Q460820|1=Iga, Mie}}Abo 1555 |Position = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = Omurakami |Divinity = |Company rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
Old {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha}} |Foundation = unknown |Main shrine = {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}}{{ill|lt=cypress bark roof|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477|1=cypress bark roof}} |Regular festival = {{ill|lt=November 2nd|Q2996|WD=Q2996|1=November 2}}・{{ill|lt=November 3rd|Q2995|WD=Q2995|lt=3rd|1=November 3}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Omura Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }}[[File:Omura-jinja (Iga) ichinotorii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ichino Torii}}]] '''Omura Shrine'' (Omura Jinja) is located in {{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Iga City|Q460820|WD=Q460820|1=Iga, Mie}} Ao. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} and {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Old company rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha}}. === Enshrined God === The following 21 deities are enshrined. . '''Main enshrined deity''' * '''Omurakami'' (Omurakami) *: In the shrine biography, the 11th {{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}{{ill|lt=Prince|Q12886381|WD=Q12886381|1=Imperial Prince}}'s {{ill|lt=Kisokubetsumei|Q11492324|WD=Q11492324|1=Prince Ikobayawake no Mikoto}} (Ikohayawake no Mikoto/Okihayawake no Mikoto) is applied to this. '''Deity enshrined'' * {{ill|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}} * {{ill|lt=Kyotsu no Mikoto|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|lt=Kyotsu no Mikoto|1=Futsunushi}} (Futsunushi no Mikoto) * {{ill|lt=Amagiya Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Amagoyane Mikoto|1=Ame-no-Koyane}} (Amenokoyane Mikoto) '''Combined deities''' * Total 17 pillars including {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}} === About the enshrined deity === The enshrined deity is listed as 1 in ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'' {{ill|lt=Engishiki divine name book|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=God name book|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. On the other hand, in the ``Kofukuji Kanjusho'' written in the first year of {{ill|lt=Kakichi|Q1193238|WD=Q1193238|1=Kakitsu}} ({{ill|lt=1441|Q6402|WD=Q6402|1=1441}}), ``Omurakami It is written as "Niza", and {{ill|lt=Kasugagami|Q11088805|WD=Q11088805|1=Kasuga-no-Kami}} was born in {{ill|lt=Jingo Keiun|Q1373049|WD=Q1373049|1=Jingo-keiun}} in 2nd year ({{ill|lt=768|Q29961|WD=Q29961|1=768}}). It is said to be the place where he rested when he moved from Jingu Shrine | Q706499}} to {{ill|lt=Mikasayama|Q10866570|WD=Q10866570|1=Mikasayama}} ({{ill|lt=Kasuga Taisha|Q714559|WD=Q714559|1=Kasuga-taisha}}) {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. The current enshrined deities are {{ill|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi no Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}}, {{ill|lt=Tsunetsu no Mikoto|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|lt=Tsunetsu no Mikoto|1=Futsunushi}}, {{jalin k|Amagiya-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|Amagoyane-no-Mikoto}} and the other three Kasuga deities originate from this transfer tradition, but its historical reality is unclear {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}.On the other hand, {{ill|lt=Bokusokubetsu no Mikoto|Q11492324|WD=Q11492324|1=Prince Ikobayawake no Mikoto}}, who is currently designated as Omura-kami, is a person whose relationship with the Abo area can be seen in ancient historical materials {{Sfn|Omura-jinja Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. First of all, in the article "{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihongi|Q611399|WD=Q611399|1=Shoku Nihongi}}" dated November 21, {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176|1=Enryaku}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=784 years|Q30083|WD=Q30083|1=784}}), the speed of breathing is {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}} who lived in Abo Village, Iga Province, and whose fourth grandson, King Sune Tsuto (Suchin Tsuto King), was given the surname "{{ill|lt=Abo clan|Q11657230|WD=Q11657230|lt=Abo-kun|1=Abo clan}}". Also, “{{ill|lt=Newly Selected Surname List|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}” by Ukyo Emperor In the Abo Ason Article, it is written that Emperor Suinin created a palace in Abo Village, Iga Province and granted it to Isokubetsu no Mikoto as a fief, and that his descendants became Abo Ason ({{ill|lt=Abo clan|Q11657230|WD=Q11657230|1=Abo clan}}) {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. Near Omura Shrine, there is also a tomb designated by the Imperial Household Agency, which according to recent archeology dates back to {{ill|lt=6th century|Q8090|WD=Q8090|1=6th century}}, so there is a theory that one of the tombs (Miyayama Tumulus Group) within the precincts of Omura Shrine is the tomb of Seisokubetsu no Mikoto.{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}.In addition to the above, there is also a theory that the enshrined deity is Yuki Isun or Daimyo Somei ({{ill|lt=Omura clan (Kamibetsu)|Q11436025|WD=Q11436025|lt=Nao Omura|1=Ōmura Clan (Shinbetsu)}} ancestor) {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. The 17 deities enshrined together are the 28 shrines of the former Abo town and 3 shrines of the former Taneo village that were enshrined between 1890 and 1986 ({{ill|lt=1890|Q7832|WD=Q7832|1=1890}}-{{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056|1=1908}}). === History === === Founding === Founding date is unknown. As mentioned above, there is a legend about the transfer of Kasuga God from 2 years {{ill|lt=Jingo Keiun|Q1373049|WD=Q1373049|1=Jingo-keiun}} ({{ill|lt=768 years|Q29961|WD=Q29961|1=768}}) (first seen in historical sources is {{ill|lt=1441|Q6402|WD=Q6402|1=1441}}) ), there is a theory that assumes the involvement of the {{ill|lt=Abo clan|Q11657230|WD=Q11657230|1=Abo clan}} (a descendant clan of the Breathless Gods) who made this place {{ill|lt=honkan|Q846706|WD=Q846706|1=Korean clans}}{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. Regarding the company's name, there is a theory that it is a corruption of ``Omori'', which refers to a group of shrines, to ``Omura'', since it looks like ``Omori Shrine/Omori Myojin'' in ancient times, or that it is a corruption of the place name ``Abo Village'', which is a corruption of ``Omura'', but the name is unknown.{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. === Outline === In {{ill|lt=Rokukokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168|lt=Kokukushi|1=Rikkokushi}}, {{ill|lt=Jōkan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}}5 ({{ill|lt=863|Q25052|WD=Q25052|1=863}}) There is a record that the {{ill|lt=jinka|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} of ``Omurakami'' was promoted from the upper rank of upper sixth rank to lower rank of junior fifth rank {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1990}}. {{ill|lt=extension (Era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extended|1=Enchō}} 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}) "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}" {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shinmyocho|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} is described as "Omura Shrine" in {{ill|lt=Iga Province|Q1047128|WD=Q1047128|1=Iga Province}}{{ill|lt=Iga District|Q11380682|WD=Q11380682|1=Iga district}}, and is listed in {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchi|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}}{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1990}}. Also, among the place names that appear in ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Japanese name extract|1=UNKNOWN}}'', the current enshrined place is thought to be Abogo, Iga District{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}.According to "Sangokujishi", the shrine suffered a fire in {{ill|lt=Eisho|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} years ({{ill|lt=1504|Q6148|WD=Q6148|1=1504}}-{{ill|lt=1521|Q6288|WD=Q6288|1=1521}}), and then in {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}9 years ({{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}). {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}} was destroyed by fire during the {{ill|lt=Tensho Iga Rebellion|Q15436846|WD=Q15436846|1=Tenshō Iga War}} in {{ill|lt=1581|Q6717|WD=Q6717|1=1581}}), but was rebuilt in Tensho 15 ({{ill|lt=1587|Q6744|WD=Q6744|1=1587}}). The main shrine built at this time still remains today as a treasure hall (national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}). In this {{ill|lt=ridge tag|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834|1=Munafuda}} dated 15th year of Tensho, the company name is "Kashima Daimyojin" and {{ill|lt=Genwa (Japan)|Q635245|WD=Q635245|lt=Genna|1=Genna}}7th year ({{ill|lt=1621|Q6812|WD=Q6812|1=1621}}), {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yasunaga|1=UNKNOWN}}8th year ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=1779|1=UNKNOWN}})'s ridge tag also appears as ``Kashima Shrine,'' so it is known that the legend of the transfer of Kasuga Shrine was well-established during this period {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Furthermore, the Akinaga clan had been in charge of the Shinto priesthood since Tensho 15 (1990).After {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, on August 7, {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 17 ({{ill|lt=1884|Q7819|WD=Q7819|1=1884}}), he was ranked as {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} in {{ill|lt=Modern Company Ranking System|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, and in Meiji 39 In December 2006 ({{ill|lt=1906|Q2049|WD=Q2049|1=1906}}), it was designated as a Shinto shrine, and in 5 years ({{j alink|1930|Q18787}}) On November 27th, it was promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}. {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1990}}. {{ill|lt=Bettoji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} was once a Zenjoji temple, but it was abandoned in the first year of the Meiji era {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. === God level === * {{ill|lt=Jogan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jogan|1=Jōgan}} 5th year ({{ill|lt=863|Q25052|WD=Q25052|1=863}}) March 16th, from upper 6th rank to lower junior fifth rank (``{{ill|lt=Japan Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}'') - Written as ``Omurakami.'' === Precincts === The current ``main shrine'' was built in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}23 ({{ill|lt=1890|Q7832|WD=Q7832|1=1890}}){{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. It is {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}}, and the roof is {{ill|lt=cypress bark|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477|1=cypress bark roof}}.{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1990}}. The old main hall, which was used before the current main hall, is adjacent to the west side of the current main hall as the ``Hoden''. This Hoden was built in the 15th year of {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} ({{ill|lt=1587|Q6744|WD=Q6744|1=1587}}) of the {{ill|lt=Azuchi-Momoyama period|Q319531|WD=Q319531|1=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}. Ikkensha {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}}, with a tsumairi roof, {{ill|lt=cypress bark|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477|1=cypress bark roof}}. It is said that the form as well as the carvings and coloring remain as they were at the time of construction, and it has been designated as a national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}.In addition, within the precincts there is a {{ill|lt=Keystone|Q11630053|WD=Q11630053|1=Kaname-ishi}}, which is worshiped as a spiritual stone, and the strange bell ``Mushikui-kane'' {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. The name "Insect-eaten bell" comes from the fact that the bumps on its surface fell off due to a chemical change, but it was originally a temple bell at Zenjoji Temple (formerly Bettoji Temple) and was completed in {{ill|lt=Meireki|Q1192766|WD=Q1192766|1=Meireki}}2 ({{ill|lt=1656|Q6982|WD=Q6982|1=1656}}) {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Also, from the approach to the shrine grounds to the vicinity of the shrine building, there are several {{ill|lt=circular tombs|Q11394747|WD=Q11394747|1=Round Kofun}} known as the ``Miyayama kofun group''{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. Old {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha}} |Foundation = unknown |Main shrine = {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}}{{ill|lt=cypress bark roof|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477|1=cypress bark roof}} |Regular festival = {{ill|lt=November 2nd|Q2996|WD=Q2996|1=November 2}}・{{ill|lt=November 3rd|Q2995|WD=Q2995|lt=3rd|1=November 3}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Omura Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }}[[File:Omura-jinja (Iga) ichinotorii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ichino Torii}}]] '''Omura Shrine'' (Omura Jinja) is located in {{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196|1=Mie Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Iga City|Q460820|WD=Q460820|1=Iga, Mie}} Ao. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} and {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Old company rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha}}. === Enshrined God === The following 21 deities are enshrined. . '''Main enshrined deity''' * '''Omurakami'' (Omurakami) *: In the shrine biography, the 11th {{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}{{ill|lt=Prince|Q12886381|WD=Q12886381|1=Imperial Prince}}'s {{ill|lt=Kisokubetsumei|Q11492324|WD=Q11492324|1=Prince Ikobayawake no Mikoto}} (Ikohayawake no Mikoto/Okihayawake no Mikoto) is applied to this. '''Deity enshrined'' * {{ill|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}} * {{ill|lt=Kyotsu no Mikoto|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|lt=Kyotsu no Mikoto|1=Futsunushi}} (Futsunushi no Mikoto) * {{ill|lt=Amagiya Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Amagoyane Mikoto|1=Ame-no-Koyane}} (Amenokoyane Mikoto) '''Combined deities''' * Total 17 pillars including {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}} === About the enshrined deity === The enshrined deity is listed as 1 in ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'' {{ill|lt=Engishiki divine name book|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=God name book|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. On the other hand, in the ``Kofukuji Kanjusho'' written in the first year of {{ill|lt=Kakichi|Q1193238|WD=Q1193238|1=Kakitsu}} ({{ill|lt=1441|Q6402|WD=Q6402|1=1441}}), ``Omurakami It is written as "Niza", and {{ill|lt=Kasugagami|Q11088805|WD=Q11088805|1=Kasuga-no-Kami}} was born in {{ill|lt=Jingo Keiun|Q1373049|WD=Q1373049|1=Jingo-keiun}} in 2nd year ({{ill|lt=768|Q29961|WD=Q29961|1=768}}). It is said to be the place where he rested when he moved from Jingu Shrine | Q706499}} to {{ill|lt=Mikasayama|Q10866570|WD=Q10866570|1=Mikasayama}} ({{ill|lt=Kasuga Taisha|Q714559|WD=Q714559|1=Kasuga-taisha}}) {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. The current enshrined deities are {{ill|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi no Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}}, {{ill|lt=Tsunetsu no Mikoto|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|lt=Tsunetsu no Mikoto|1=Futsunushi}}, {{jalin k|Amagiya-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|Amagoyane-no-Mikoto}} and the other three Kasuga deities originate from this transfer tradition, but its historical reality is unclear {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}.On the other hand, {{ill|lt=Bokusokubetsu no Mikoto|Q11492324|WD=Q11492324|1=Prince Ikobayawake no Mikoto}}, who is currently designated as Omura-kami, is a person whose relationship with the Abo area can be seen in ancient historical materials {{Sfn|Omura-jinja Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. First of all, in the article "{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihongi|Q611399|WD=Q611399|1=Shoku Nihongi}}" dated November 21, {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176|1=Enryaku}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=784 years|Q30083|WD=Q30083|1=784}}), the speed of breathing is {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}} who lived in Abo Village, Iga Province, and whose fourth grandson, King Sune Tsuto (Suchin Tsuto King), was given the surname "{{ill|lt=Abo clan|Q11657230|WD=Q11657230|lt=Abo-kun|1=Abo clan}}". Also, “{{ill|lt=Newly Selected Surname List|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}” by Ukyo Emperor In the Abo Ason Article, it is written that Emperor Suinin created a palace in Abo Village, Iga Province and granted it to Isokubetsu no Mikoto as a fief, and that his descendants became Abo Ason ({{ill|lt=Abo clan|Q11657230|WD=Q11657230|1=Abo clan}}) {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. Near Omura Shrine, there is also a tomb designated by the Imperial Household Agency, which according to recent archeology dates back to {{ill|lt=6th century|Q8090|WD=Q8090|1=6th century}}, so there is a theory that one of the tombs (Miyayama Tumulus Group) within the precincts of Omura Shrine is the tomb of Seisokubetsu no Mikoto.{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}.In addition to the above, there is also a theory that the enshrined deity is Yuki Isun or Daimyo Somei ({{ill|lt=Omura clan (Kamibetsu)|Q11436025|WD=Q11436025|lt=Nao Omura|1=Ōmura Clan (Shinbetsu)}} ancestor) {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. The 17 deities enshrined together are the 28 shrines of the former Abo town and 3 shrines of the former Taneo village that were enshrined between 1890 and 1986 ({{ill|lt=1890|Q7832|WD=Q7832|1=1890}}-{{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056|1=1908}}). === History === === Founding === Founding date is unknown. As mentioned above, there is a legend about the transfer of Kasuga God from 2 years {{ill|lt=Jingo Keiun|Q1373049|WD=Q1373049|1=Jingo-keiun}} ({{ill|lt=768 years|Q29961|WD=Q29961|1=768}}) (first seen in historical sources is {{ill|lt=1441|Q6402|WD=Q6402|1=1441}}) ), there is a theory that assumes the involvement of the {{ill|lt=Abo clan|Q11657230|WD=Q11657230|1=Abo clan}} (a descendant clan of the Breathless Gods) who made this place {{ill|lt=honkan|Q846706|WD=Q846706|1=Korean clans}}{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. Regarding the company's name, there is a theory that it is a corruption of ``Omori'', which refers to a group of shrines, to ``Omura'', since it looks like ``Omori Shrine/Omori Myojin'' in ancient times, or that it is a corruption of the place name ``Abo Village'', which is a corruption of ``Omura'', but the name is unknown.{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. === Outline === In {{ill|lt=Rokukokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168|lt=Kokukushi|1=Rikkokushi}}, {{ill|lt=Jōkan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}}5 ({{ill|lt=863|Q25052|WD=Q25052|1=863}}) There is a record that the {{ill|lt=jinka|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} of ``Omurakami'' was promoted from the upper rank of upper sixth rank to lower rank of junior fifth rank {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1990}}. {{ill|lt=extension (Era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extended|1=Enchō}} 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}) "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}" {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shinmyocho|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} is described as "Omura Shrine" in {{ill|lt=Iga Province|Q1047128|WD=Q1047128|1=Iga Province}}{{ill|lt=Iga District|Q11380682|WD=Q11380682|1=Iga district}}, and is listed in {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchi|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}}{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1990}}. Also, among the place names that appear in ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Japanese name extract|1=UNKNOWN}}'', the current enshrined place is thought to be Abogo, Iga District{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}.According to "Sangokujishi", the shrine suffered a fire in {{ill|lt=Eisho|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} years ({{ill|lt=1504|Q6148|WD=Q6148|1=1504}}-{{ill|lt=1521|Q6288|WD=Q6288|1=1521}}), and then in {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}9 years ({{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}). {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}} was destroyed by fire during the {{ill|lt=Tensho Iga Rebellion|Q15436846|WD=Q15436846|1=Tenshō Iga War}} in {{ill|lt=1581|Q6717|WD=Q6717|1=1581}}), but was rebuilt in Tensho 15 ({{ill|lt=1587|Q6744|WD=Q6744|1=1587}}). The main shrine built at this time still remains today as a treasure hall (national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}). In this {{ill|lt=ridge tag|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834|1=Munafuda}} dated 15th year of Tensho, the company name is "Kashima Daimyojin" and {{ill|lt=Genwa (Japan)|Q635245|WD=Q635245|lt=Genna|1=Genna}}7th year ({{ill|lt=1621|Q6812|WD=Q6812|1=1621}}), {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yasunaga|1=UNKNOWN}}8th year ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=1779|1=UNKNOWN}})'s ridge tag also appears as ``Kashima Shrine,'' so it is known that the legend of the transfer of Kasuga Shrine was well-established during this period {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Furthermore, the Akinaga clan had been in charge of the Shinto priesthood since Tensho 15 (1990).After {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, on August 7, {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 17 ({{ill|lt=1884|Q7819|WD=Q7819|1=1884}}), he was ranked as {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} in {{ill|lt=Modern Company Ranking System|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, and in Meiji 39 In December 2006 ({{ill|lt=1906|Q2049|WD=Q2049|1=1906}}), it was designated as a Shinto shrine, and in 5 years ({{j alink|1930|Q18787}}) On November 27th, it was promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}. {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1990}}. {{ill|lt=Bettoji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} was once a Zenjoji temple, but it was abandoned in the first year of the Meiji era {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. === God level === * {{ill|lt=Jogan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jogan|1=Jōgan}} 5th year ({{ill|lt=863|Q25052|WD=Q25052|1=863}}) March 16th, from upper 6th rank to lower junior fifth rank (``{{ill|lt=Japan Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}'') - Written as ``Omurakami.'' === Precincts === The current ``main shrine'' was built in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}23 ({{ill|lt=1890|Q7832|WD=Q7832|1=1890}}){{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. It is {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}}, and the roof is {{ill|lt=cypress bark|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477|1=cypress bark roof}}.{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1990}}. The old main hall, which was used before the current main hall, is adjacent to the west side of the current main hall as the ``Hoden''. This Hoden was built in the 15th year of {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} ({{ill|lt=1587|Q6744|WD=Q6744|1=1587}}) of the {{ill|lt=Azuchi-Momoyama period|Q319531|WD=Q319531|1=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}. Ikkensha {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}}, with a tsumairi roof, {{ill|lt=cypress bark|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477|1=cypress bark roof}}. It is said that the form as well as the carvings and coloring remain as they were at the time of construction, and it has been designated as a national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}.In addition, within the precincts there is a {{ill|lt=Keystone|Q11630053|WD=Q11630053|1=Kaname-ishi}}, which is worshiped as a spiritual stone, and the strange bell ``Mushikui-kane'' {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. The name "Insect-eaten bell" comes from the fact that the bumps on its surface fell off due to a chemical change, but it was originally a temple bell at Zenjoji Temple (formerly Bettoji Temple) and was completed in {{ill|lt=Meireki|Q1192766|WD=Q1192766|1=Meireki}}2 ({{ill|lt=1656|Q6982|WD=Q6982|1=1656}}) {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Also, from the approach to the shrine grounds to the vicinity of the shrine building, there are several {{ill|lt=circular tombs|Q11394747|WD=Q11394747|1=Round Kofun}} known as the ``Miyayama kofun group''{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. '''Main enshrined deity''' * '''Omurakami'' (Omurakami) *: In the shrine biography, the 11th {{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}{{ill|lt=Prince|Q12886381|WD=Q12886381|1=Imperial Prince}}'s {{ill|lt=Kisokubetsumei|Q11492324|WD=Q11492324|1=Prince Ikobayawake no Mikoto}} (Ikohayawake no Mikoto/Okihayawake no Mikoto) is applied to this. '''Deity enshrined'' * {{ill|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}} * {{ill|lt=Kyotsu no Mikoto|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|lt=Kyotsu no Mikoto|1=Futsunushi}} (Futsunushi no Mikoto) * {{ill|lt=Amagiya Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Amagoyane Mikoto|1=Ame-no-Koyane}} (Amenokoyane Mikoto) '''Combined deities''' * Total 17 pillars including {{ill|lt=Emperor Ojin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}} === About the enshrined deity === The enshrined deity is listed as 1 in ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}'' {{ill|lt=Engishiki divine name book|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=God name book|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. On the other hand, in the ``Kofukuji Kanjusho'' written in the first year of {{ill|lt=Kakichi|Q1193238|WD=Q1193238|1=Kakitsu}} ({{ill|lt=1441|Q6402|WD=Q6402|1=1441}}), ``Omurakami It is written as "Niza", and {{ill|lt=Kasugagami|Q11088805|WD=Q11088805|1=Kasuga-no-Kami}} was born in {{ill|lt=Jingo Keiun|Q1373049|WD=Q1373049|1=Jingo-keiun}} in 2nd year ({{ill|lt=768|Q29961|WD=Q29961|1=768}}). It is said to be the place where he rested when he moved from Jingu Shrine | Q706499}} to {{ill|lt=Mikasayama|Q10866570|WD=Q10866570|1=Mikasayama}} ({{ill|lt=Kasuga Taisha|Q714559|WD=Q714559|1=Kasuga-taisha}}) {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. The current enshrined deities are {{ill|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi no Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}}, {{ill|lt=Tsunetsu no Mikoto|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|lt=Tsunetsu no Mikoto|1=Futsunushi}}, {{jalin k|Amagiya-no-Mikoto|Q2564508|Amagoyane-no-Mikoto}} and the other three Kasuga deities originate from this transfer tradition, but its historical reality is unclear {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}.On the other hand, {{ill|lt=Bokusokubetsu no Mikoto|Q11492324|WD=Q11492324|1=Prince Ikobayawake no Mikoto}}, who is currently designated as Omura-kami, is a person whose relationship with the Abo area can be seen in ancient historical materials {{Sfn|Omura-jinja Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. First of all, in the article "{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihongi|Q611399|WD=Q611399|1=Shoku Nihongi}}" dated November 21, {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|WD=Q1373176|1=Enryaku}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=784 years|Q30083|WD=Q30083|1=784}}), the speed of breathing is {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}} who lived in Abo Village, Iga Province, and whose fourth grandson, King Sune Tsuto (Suchin Tsuto King), was given the surname "{{ill|lt=Abo clan|Q11657230|WD=Q11657230|lt=Abo-kun|1=Abo clan}}". Also, “{{ill|lt=Newly Selected Surname List|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}” by Ukyo Emperor In the Abo Ason Article, it is written that Emperor Suinin created a palace in Abo Village, Iga Province and granted it to Isokubetsu no Mikoto as a fief, and that his descendants became Abo Ason ({{ill|lt=Abo clan|Q11657230|WD=Q11657230|1=Abo clan}}) {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. Near Omura Shrine, there is also a tomb designated by the Imperial Household Agency, which according to recent archeology dates back to {{ill|lt=6th century|Q8090|WD=Q8090|1=6th century}}, so there is a theory that one of the tombs (Miyayama Tumulus Group) within the precincts of Omura Shrine is the tomb of Seisokubetsu no Mikoto.{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}.In addition to the above, there is also a theory that the enshrined deity is Yuki Isun or Daimyo Somei ({{ill|lt=Omura clan (Kamibetsu)|Q11436025|WD=Q11436025|lt=Nao Omura|1=Ōmura Clan (Shinbetsu)}} ancestor) {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. The 17 deities enshrined together are the 28 shrines of the former Abo town and 3 shrines of the former Taneo village that were enshrined between 1890 and 1986 ({{ill|lt=1890|Q7832|WD=Q7832|1=1890}}-{{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056|1=1908}}). === History === === Founding === Founding date is unknown. As mentioned above, there is a legend about the transfer of Kasuga God from 2 years {{ill|lt=Jingo Keiun|Q1373049|WD=Q1373049|1=Jingo-keiun}} ({{ill|lt=768 years|Q29961|WD=Q29961|1=768}}) (first seen in historical sources is {{ill|lt=1441|Q6402|WD=Q6402|1=1441}}) ), there is a theory that assumes the involvement of the {{ill|lt=Abo clan|Q11657230|WD=Q11657230|1=Abo clan}} (a descendant clan of the Breathless Gods) who made this place {{ill|lt=honkan|Q846706|WD=Q846706|1=Korean clans}}{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. Regarding the company's name, there is a theory that it is a corruption of ``Omori'', which refers to a group of shrines, to ``Omura'', since it looks like ``Omori Shrine/Omori Myojin'' in ancient times, or that it is a corruption of the place name ``Abo Village'', which is a corruption of ``Omura'', but the name is unknown.{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}. === Outline === In {{ill|lt=Rokukokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168|lt=Kokukushi|1=Rikkokushi}}, {{ill|lt=Jōkan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}}5 ({{ill|lt=863|Q25052|WD=Q25052|1=863}}) There is a record that the {{ill|lt=jinka|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} of ``Omurakami'' was promoted from the upper rank of upper sixth rank to lower rank of junior fifth rank {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1990}}. {{ill|lt=extension (Era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extended|1=Enchō}} 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}) "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}" {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shinmyocho|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} is described as "Omura Shrine" in {{ill|lt=Iga Province|Q1047128|WD=Q1047128|1=Iga Province}}{{ill|lt=Iga District|Q11380682|WD=Q11380682|1=Iga district}}, and is listed in {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchi|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}}{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1990}}. Also, among the place names that appear in ``{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Japanese name extract|1=UNKNOWN}}'', the current enshrined place is thought to be Abogo, Iga District{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}.According to "Sangokujishi", the shrine suffered a fire in {{ill|lt=Eisho|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577|1=Eishō (Muromachi period)}} years ({{ill|lt=1504|Q6148|WD=Q6148|1=1504}}-{{ill|lt=1521|Q6288|WD=Q6288|1=1521}}), and then in {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}9 years ({{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}). {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}} was destroyed by fire during the {{ill|lt=Tensho Iga Rebellion|Q15436846|WD=Q15436846|1=Tenshō Iga War}} in {{ill|lt=1581|Q6717|WD=Q6717|1=1581}}), but was rebuilt in Tensho 15 ({{ill|lt=1587|Q6744|WD=Q6744|1=1587}}). The main shrine built at this time still remains today as a treasure hall (national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}). In this {{ill|lt=ridge tag|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834|1=Munafuda}} dated 15th year of Tensho, the company name is "Kashima Daimyojin" and {{ill|lt=Genwa (Japan)|Q635245|WD=Q635245|lt=Genna|1=Genna}}7th year ({{ill|lt=1621|Q6812|WD=Q6812|1=1621}}), {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yasunaga|1=UNKNOWN}}8th year ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=1779|1=UNKNOWN}})'s ridge tag also appears as ``Kashima Shrine,'' so it is known that the legend of the transfer of Kasuga Shrine was well-established during this period {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Furthermore, the Akinaga clan had been in charge of the Shinto priesthood since Tensho 15 (1990).After {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, on August 7, {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 17 ({{ill|lt=1884|Q7819|WD=Q7819|1=1884}}), he was ranked as {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} in {{ill|lt=Modern Company Ranking System|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, and in Meiji 39 In December 2006 ({{ill|lt=1906|Q2049|WD=Q2049|1=1906}}), it was designated as a Shinto shrine, and in 5 years ({{j alink|1930|Q18787}}) On November 27th, it was promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}. {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1990}}. {{ill|lt=Bettoji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} was once a Zenjoji temple, but it was abandoned in the first year of the Meiji era {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. === God level === * {{ill|lt=Jogan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jogan|1=Jōgan}} 5th year ({{ill|lt=863|Q25052|WD=Q25052|1=863}}) March 16th, from upper 6th rank to lower junior fifth rank (``{{ill|lt=Japan Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}'') - Written as ``Omurakami.'' === Precincts === The current ``main shrine'' was built in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}23 ({{ill|lt=1890|Q7832|WD=Q7832|1=1890}}){{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. It is {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}}, and the roof is {{ill|lt=cypress bark|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477|1=cypress bark roof}}.{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1990}}. The old main hall, which was used before the current main hall, is adjacent to the west side of the current main hall as the ``Hoden''. This Hoden was built in the 15th year of {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} ({{ill|lt=1587|Q6744|WD=Q6744|1=1587}}) of the {{ill|lt=Azuchi-Momoyama period|Q319531|WD=Q319531|1=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}. Ikkensha {{ill|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}}, with a tsumairi roof, {{ill|lt=cypress bark|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477|1=cypress bark roof}}. It is said that the form as well as the carvings and coloring remain as they were at the time of construction, and it has been designated as a national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}.In addition, within the precincts there is a {{ill|lt=Keystone|Q11630053|WD=Q11630053|1=Kaname-ishi}}, which is worshiped as a spiritual stone, and the strange bell ``Mushikui-kane'' {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. The name "Insect-eaten bell" comes from the fact that the bumps on its surface fell off due to a chemical change, but it was originally a temple bell at Zenjoji Temple (formerly Bettoji Temple) and was completed in {{ill|lt=Meireki|Q1192766|WD=Q1192766|1=Meireki}}2 ({{ill|lt=1656|Q6982|WD=Q6982|1=1656}}) {{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Also, from the approach to the shrine grounds to the vicinity of the shrine building, there are several {{ill|lt=circular tombs|Q11394747|WD=Q11394747|1=Round Kofun}} known as the ``Miyayama kofun group''{{Sfn|Omura Shrine (Gods)|1986}}.
